notes.note: An estate near Puteoli that Cicero inherited from the banker Marcus Cluvius, who had named Cicero among his heirs; no separate place entity is registered, so the name is bound to its eponymous former owner.
A Roman date counted back inclusively from the Nones; here the fifth day before the Nones of May, i.e. 3 May 44 BC.
The villa of Atticus's wife Pilia sat on the Lucrine lagoon between Cumae and Puteoli; Cicero, departing by boat, is handing it over with its bailiffs and agents.
L. Papirius Paetus, Cicero's wealthy literary friend at Naples; the comic Greek word tyrotarichum ("cheese-and-salt-fish") jokes at the modest fare Cicero expects at his table.

notes.anchor: a crown of gold through the Forum
notes.note: Cicero's hyperbole that Brutus is now so popular he could parade a triumphal gold crown openly, given the public approval following Dolabella's suppression of the Caesar-cult.
Cicero's recurring plan in these weeks to sail to Greece, ostensibly to oversee his son's studies at Athens, in part to escape the political danger at home.
Leonides, an Athenian teacher supervising young Marcus's studies; Cicero reads his guarded praise of the boy as a sign of anxiety rather than confidence.
Cicero had told the rhetorician Herodes to report on his son in detail (Greek kata miton, "thread by thread," i.e. point by point); Herodes' silence worries him.

A man owing money in connection with Montanus's debts; Cicero presses Atticus to extract payment before it falls due late and at a loss. Identification with the Flamma of the financial letters is probable but not certain.
L. Tullius Montanus, a young man who had gone to Athens with Cicero's son; Cicero had stood surety for him and was now entangled in recovering the resulting debts.
